data.title: Banks vs. Crypto battle escalates over token yields
data.description:
A regulatory clash between traditional banks and the cryptocurrency industry emerges as stablecoins and other digital tokens that offer attractive yields—often higher than what banks pay on deposits.

Crypto platforms such as Coinbase Global (COIN) have been offering rewards of up to ~3.5% annual yield on certain stablecoins. These rewards are significantly above typical bank deposit rates, averaging below 0.1% for a standard interest-bearing checking account.

Under the Guiding and Establishing National Innovation for U.S. Stablecoins ((GENIUS)) Act, banks push to restrict these yields, echoing prior stablecoin bills that banned direct interest but left room for third-party rewards. 

Banking groups have flooded lawmakers with letters and calls, warning that high-yield crypto tokens could drain deposits and threaten lenders. Executives from JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Citigroup (C) have joined the push to regulate or limit crypto rewards to protect traditional banking, a WSJ report pointed out.

"The prohibition on stable coin yield does absolutely nothing, full stop.... The primary way in which platforms fund yield is through activities like staking and on lending, which are explicitly carved out of the prohibition. So this is language that has the veil of banning yield on stablecoins, but doesn't actually." CoinDesk quoted Corey Frayer, a crypto adviser for former U.S. SEC Chair Gary Gensler who is now at the Consumer Federation of America.

data.title: Ericsson proposes to cut 1,600 jobs in Sweden
data.description:
• Ericsson (ERIC) has announced proposed staff reductions in Sweden as part of measures aimed at ensuring the company's competitive position.
  • The telecommunications company has submitted a notice to the Swedish Public Employment Service and initiated talks with the relevant Swedish trade unions. Around 1,600 positions could be impacted in Sweden.
  • “The proposed staff reduction is part of global initiatives to improve cost position while maintaining investments critical to Ericsson's technology leadership and the execution of the strategy to deliver high-performing, programmable networks that enable differentiated services and new monetization opportunities,” the firm said.
  • Initiatives to increase operational efficiency will continue across the group but will not be announced separately, it added.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Germany’s GDP growth turns positive at 0.1% in 2025
data.description:
Full-year GDP growth in Germany increased to 0.1% in 2025 from -0.5% in 2024.

data.title: Switzerland probes Microsoft over rising licensing fees
data.description:
• Switzerland’s competition authority has launched a preliminary probe into Microsoft (MSFT) regarding the licensing fees it charges local clients, following complaints about recent price hikes.
  • The “significant” price hikes for Microsoft 365 will be examined to see whether they constitute “an unlawful restriction of competition,” the regulator known as Comco said in a statement on Wednesday.
  • "Should such indications be confirmed, this could warrant the opening of a formal investigation. Consideration may be given to involving the expertise of the Price Supervision Authority," the statement said.

data.title: Elon Musk's xAI blocks Grok from undressing images after global backlash
data.description:
Elon Musk's xAI (X.AI) said it implemented technological measures to prevent AI chatbot Grok from editing images of real people in revealing clothing such as bikinis, and the restriction will apply to all users, including paid subscribers.

"Additionally, image creation and the ability to edit images via the Grok account on the X platform are now only available to paid subscribers," xAI (X.AI) said, adding that this will ensure that individuals who try to abuse Grok to violate its policies or the law can be held accountable.

"We now geoblock the ability of all users to generate images of real people in bikinis, underwear, and similar attire via the Grok account and in Grok in X in those jurisdictions where it's illegal," it added.

The updates follow global outrage over people creating nonconsensual deepfakes through Grok and prompting the chatbot to digitally undress images of adults, and in some cases, children.

Musk, who is also the CEO of Tesla (TSLA) said he was not aware of any naked underage images generated by Grok. 

"When asked to generate images, it will refuse to produce anything illegal, as the operating principle for Grok is to obey the laws of any given country or state," he clarified. "There may be times when adversarial hacking of Grok prompts does something unexpected. If that happens, we fix the bug immediately."

"With NSFW [not safe for work] enabled, Grok is supposed [to] allow upper body nudity of imaginary adult humans (not real ones) consistent with what can be seen in R-rated movies on Apple TV," Musk later said. "That is the de facto standard in America. This will vary in other regions according to the laws on a country by country basis."

data.title: Europe indexes mixed after flurry of economic indicators
data.description:
London (UKX) -0.06% to 10,177. UK's GDP expands in November. UK construction output declined 1.1% Y/Y in November. Manufacturing production in the UK grew by 2.1% M/M in November.

Germany (DAX:IND) -0.18% to 25,239. Germany’s wholesale prices rose 1.2% Y/Y in December.

France (CAC:IND) +0.02% to 8,330. France's inflation rate drops to 0.80% in December.

In other parts of Europe, Spain's inflation drops to 2.9% in December, meeting estimates

The industrial production in Hungary fell by 5.4% Y/Y in November.

Retail sales in the Czech Republic rose by 4.6% Y/Y in November.

Norway’s industrial confidence advanced to 2.6 in Q4. Norway's trade surplus narrowed to NOK42.9B in December.

Sweden’s annual inflation rate stood at 0.3% in December.

The pan-European Stoxx 600 (STOXX) traded 0.24% higher around €613, 

In the bond market, the U.S. 10-year Treasury yield was up 1 basis point to 4.15%. 

Germany's 10-year yield was up 1 basis point to 2.83%.

UK's 10-year yield was up 3 basis points to 4.37%.

Currencies: (EUR:USD) (GBP:USD) (CHF:USD)

data.title: U.S. to pause immigrant visas for 75 countries
data.description:
The U.S. State Department said Wednesday it will suspend the processing of immigrant visas for citizens of 75 countries, including Afghanistan, Iran, Russia, and Somalia.

In a post on X, the administration said the pause applies to countries whose migrants “take welfare from the American people at unacceptable rates,” adding that the freeze will remain in place until the U.S. can ensure that “new immigrants will not extract wealth from the American people.”

Effective January 21, 2026, immigrant visa issuances will be paused for applicants who are nationals of the affected countries, which span regions across Africa, Asia, Europe, Latin America, and the Caribbean.

The countries include: Afghanistan, Albania, Algeria, Antigua and Barbuda, Armenia, Azerbaijan, Bahamas, Bangladesh, Barbados, Belarus, Belize, Bhutan, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Brazil, Burma, Cambodia, Cameroon, Cape Verde, Colombia, Cote d’Ivoire, Cuba, Democratic Republic of the Congo, Dominica, Egypt, Eritrea, Ethiopia, Fiji, The Gambia, Georgia, Ghana, Grenada, Guatemala, Guinea, Haiti, Iran, Iraq, Jamaica, Jordan, Kazakhstan, Kosovo, Kuwait, Kyrgyz Republic, Laos, Lebanon, Liberia, Libya, Moldova, Mongolia, Montenegro, Morocco, Nepal, Nicaragua, Nigeria, North Macedonia, Pakistan, Republic of the Congo, Russia, Rwanda, Saint Kitts and Nevis, Saint Lucia, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, Senegal, Sierra Leone, Somalia, South Sudan, Sudan, Syria, Tanzania, Thailand, Togo, Tunisia, Uganda, Uruguay, Uzbekistan, and Yemen

The move follows a series of immigration and travel restrictions under President Donald Trump, including the suspension of the diversity visa program in late December. That program had allowed up to 55,000 immigrants annually from countries with historically low rates of U.S. immigration.

Earlier this week, the State Department said it has revoked more than 100,000 visas, including roughly 8,000 student visas and 2,500 specialized visas held by individuals who had encounters with U.S. law enforcement involving criminal activity.

The administration has also tightened rules around skilled-worker visas. In September, Trump imposed a $100,000 annual fee on H-1B visas, dealing a blow to companies—particularly in the technology and financial sectors—that rely on the program to recruit foreign talent.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Spain's inflation drops to 2.9% in December, meeting estimates
France's inflation rate drops to 0.80% in December
The UK's GDP expands in November,
data.description:
• The UK economy expanded by 0.3% M/M in November 2025, rebounding from a 0.1% contraction in October and outperforming market expectations of 0.1% growth.
  • Real GDP per head is estimated to have shown no growth in the latest quarter and is up 0.9% compared with the same quarter a year ago. Monthly GDP increased to 1.40% Y/Y in November from 1.1% in October. 
  • The value of goods imports decreased by £0.6B (1.1%) in November, with a fall in imports from non-EU countries partially offset by a rise in imports from the EU.
  • The goods trade balance in the UK increased to -£23710M in November from -£24170M in October of 2025.
